observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: a. Inventory Committee in coordination with the GSO, the Municipal Engineer, and the MPDC, (i) complete the physical count of all properties of the Municipality including all road infrastructures and prepare the RPCPPE and RPCLRN; (ii) in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, expedite reconciliation of the inventory reports with the accounting records, taking into consideration the deficiencies noted in audit; and (iii) submit the final copies thereof to the Audit Team; b. Municipal GSO (i) maintain a complete and updated PCs and LRNPC to facilitate the reconciliation; (ii) prepare and submit the RLRN at the end of the accounting period, as required under Annex A of COA Circular No. 2015-008, ensuring the report includes comprehensive details; (iii) coordinate with the Accounting Office to comply with the procedural requirements on the reclassification and recognition of semi-expendable properties and provide a copy of the RSPI to the Accounting Office; and (iv) initiate the proper disposal of all unserviceable properties and complete the information on the IIRUP for submission to the Accounting Office; c. Require the Municipal Engineer to submit a complete report on the costs, description and specifications and cost segregation of the road components to the Municipal Accountant as basis for disclosures in the NTFS; and d. Municipal Accountant to (i) upon receipt of the RPCLRN and RLRN, initiate reconciliation with the SLs for accurate financial reporting; (ii) determine the unidentified PPE for proper valuation and allocation of its cost against its estimated useful life; (iii) determine, reclassify and provide for the revised estimated useful life for the PPE items acquired at lump-sum prices based on the cost breakdown or relative fair value; (iv) provide disclosure in the NTFS for the road network system, including length, value, additions, reductions, and other movements during the year; and (v) reconcile the derecognized PPE against the RSPI provided by the GSO.
observations.managementAction: RPCPPE was prepared in CY 2024. physical count of immovable assets. The RPCLRN was not prepared while the LRNLC was not maintained contrary to Sections 119 and 124 of the MNGAS. The Municipal Accountant determined unidentified PPE totaling ₱8,004,990.60 and charged PPE items below threshold in accordance with COA Circulars. The Municipal GSO will coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the results of the physical inventory with the SLs.
observations.reason: According to the Municipal GSO, the physical count of PPE is nearly completed, except for road infrastructure assets, due to the unavailability of necessary data from the Municipal Engineering Office. To address this, the GSO and the Municipal Engineer, in close coordination, will conduct the physical count of immovable assets. The GSO will coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the results of the physical inventory with the SLs. As to the identified ROW properties, the Management clarified that out of the four (4) identified, only two (2) are considered for possible transfer of Tax Declarations under the name of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. GSO and Inventory Committee to (a) request for a copy of the SLs maintained by the Municipal Accountant as basis for the items to be counted; (b) complete the physical count of all inventoriable items; (c) prepare the RPCI and RSPI; (d) submit the reports to the Audit Team for evaluation and to the Municipal Accountant for proper recording in the books; b. MDRRMO, MHO, GSO and other concerned offices to (i) immediately submit the required distribution/consumption/utilization reports of inventories as well as the inventory of undistributed/unissued goods/items; and (ii) render satisfactory explanation on the delayed reporting of the consumption or utilization of the Medical, Dental, and Laboratory Supplies Inventory in connection with the COVID-19 operations of the LGU; c. Municipal GSO (i) ensure that issuances of supplies and materials are supported with duly signed RIS to establish accountability on the part of the end-users, and submit to the Municipal Accountant the monthly SSMI duly supported by the RIS as basis for recording expenses; and (ii) submit the monthly fuel consumption reports and ensure all FOL certifications are properly reviewed and supported; d. Municipal Accountant, (i) upon submission of the required summary report, distribution list and the inventory report by the concerned offices, make the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balances of the Inventory accounts; (ii) ensure that the Supplies Ledger Card (SLC) is properly maintained; and (iii) adjusting entries be made to record the inventories to its proper accounts in accordance with Sections 120, 121 and 122 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I and COA Circular Nos. 2022-004 and 2024-006; and e. GSO and the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the procured, issued and physically counted inventoriable items against their respective records and reports.
observations.managementAction: The GSO and the Municipal Accountant confirmed their commitment to reconcile the inventory records. The GSO reported that the physical count of inventories has been completed and that they are currently preparing the SSMIs on a weekly basis, with corresponding issued RIS. The MHO stated that the unaccounted inventories pertained to the expired test kits. The MDRRMO attributed the delays in the submission of fuel reports to the resignation of the previously assigned Contract of Service (COS) personnel. The GSO committed to submit the revised FOL Certifications duly verified by their Office to the Audit Team. The Municipal Accountant stated that accounting adjustments will be made in year 2025 for the Agricultural and Marine Supplies Inventory account under the TF, amounting to ₱435,125.00, along with other necessary corrections.
observations.reason: There are still unliquidated and unsubmitted supporting documents and SSMIs for various inventories. The GSO's supplied RPCI was incomplete. The FOL certifications provided by the gas station are considered unreliable due to unreconciled and inconsistent records. Some adjustments are planned for 2025; as of reporting, certain inventories remain unverified and unreconciled.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: a. Municipal GSO (i) together with the Municipal Assessor and Municipal Legal Officer, coordinate with the Registry of Deeds and other relevant government agencies to expedite the processing and issuance of TCTs for all parcels of land acquired by the Municipality that remain undocumented; (ii) submit to the Audit Team all the supporting documents of the 20 undocumented parcels of land; and copies of the Deed of Absolute Sale/Donation and the status of the proceedings instituted for the issuance of TCTs and TDs under the name of the municipal government for the land totaling ₱15,840,864.34; and (iii) furnish the Municipal Accountant with pertinent documents of the land for updating in the books; and b. Municipal Accountant (i) update the real property ledger cards with complete details/information for each real property acquired by the Municipality in accordance with Section 15 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II; (ii) submit a copy of the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) entered into with the DOT and DPWH on the constructed facilities on the disputed land; (iii) record and update the Land account upon submission of the pertinent documents from the GSO and the Municipal Assessor; and (iv) ensure appropriate disclosure of the matter in the NTFS in compliance with IPSAS 19.
observations.managementAction: The GSO, the Legal Officer, the Municipal Assessor, and the Accounting Office will coordinate with each other, as well as with the relevant government agencies, to facilitate the documentation of the land and submit the same to the Audit Team. Management clarified that no MOA was executed with the DOT and the DPWH regarding the facilities constructed by them on the disputed land.
observations.reason: As of reporting, only some parcels' titling work is ongoing; many parcels remain undocumented and RPLC lacked essential details. The disputed lot remains subject to third-party claims; no MOA with DOT/DPWH was provided.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor request the SB to revisit and update the existing ordinance on the daily wage rate of JO and COS personnel to ensure alignment with the prevailing minimum wage rate.
observations.managementAction: Discussions had already been held with the Municipal Mayor on the proposed adjustments to the daily wage rates of JO and COS personnel to align with the prevailing minimum wage. Such new rates will be considered in the enactment of the new ordinance.
observations.reason: No new ordinance enacted yet; wage adjustments pending SB action and new ordinance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer (a) refrain from reverting to the surplus of the GF all savings of implemented/discontinued projects under the 20% DF; (b) return to the 20% DF all reverted appropriation funded in years 2014 to 2022; (c) treat the returned/reverted/corrected amounts as unprogrammed fund subject to disposition of the Municipal Development Council; (d) rectify the fund deficiency for want of funds actually available amounting to ₱1,800,000.00 for Supplemental Budget No. 3, series of 2021 subject to the usual budgeting procedure; and (e) determine any remaining fund of Supplemental Budget No. 1, series of 2021 that will be the basis for the return of ₱586,376.59 fund deficit of the 20% DF.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Budget Officer acknowledged lapses in reverting savings to GF surplus and explained discrepancies were due to lack of proper verification and differences between Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant records. The Municipal Accountant noted inconsistencies were due to obligated projects not actually implemented. Both offices committed to stricter verification and coordination.
observations.reason: Returned/reverted funds amounting to ₱34,944,140.57 remain unprogrammed; fund deficiency of ₱1,800,000.00 and unidentified ₱586,376.59 remain unresolved pending reconciliation and correction.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Engineer inspect the projects covered within the defects liability and warranty periods and issue the Certificate of Final Acceptance. Using the Certificate of Final Acceptance, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant impose the warranty security from the contractor on the identified projects still covered by the one (1) year validity period in accordance with Section 62.2.3.4 of the updated 2016 R-IRR of RA No. 9184. Henceforth, we recommended that the Management impose warranty security on implemented projects upon its final acceptance.
observations.managementAction: Management acknowledged the audit observation and committed to comply with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Although commitment given to comply and inspections to be conducted, warranty securities were not yet imposed on identified projects; retention money was withdrawn prematurely in many cases and substituted with surety bonds of inadequate amount/term.

observations.managementAction: Details of unsettled suspensions and disallowances provided in the report with follow-up actions noted.
observations.reason: Significant unsettled suspensions and disallowances remain; several items from prior years still unresolved as detailed in the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to complete the undertaking of the one-time cleansing of the PPE accounts of the LGU, as well as the preparation and submission of the relevant reports in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 and COA Circular No. 2023-004.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee will be reconstituted and the one-time cleansing is planned. The GSO reported progress and coordination with the Accounting Office for reconciliation.
observations.reason: One-time cleansing deferred; GSO and other offices reported work-in-progress; final reconciliation and reports not yet submitted.

We recommended that the:

a. Municipal Accountant (i) accordingly record the reconciling items that need
adjustment in the books; (ii) submit the DVs and the related supporting
documents of the unrecorded check disbursements reported in the BRS; and
(iii) formulate a clear cut-off policies on month-end closing with checklist of
reports needed, e.g., cancelled and unreleased checks, from the Municipal
Treasurer before finalizing the monthly BRS;

b. Municipal Treasurer (i) provide the Audit Team with a proof that the DVs,
Obligation Requests (ObRs) and supporting documents of the unrecorded
disbursements were already submitted to the Accounting Office, otherwise
exhaust all means to locate and secure the missing transaction documents and
immediately submit to the Municipal Accountant to facilitate recording
thereof in the books of accounts; and (ii) ensure the reconciliation of the
cashbooks with the subsidiary accounting records every end of the month or
as the need arises in accordance with the provisions of Section 63(F) of the
LTOM; and

c. Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant reconcile their records as
at December 31, 2024, take-up the necessary adjusting entries in their
respective books and submit to the Audit Team a reconciliation statement
thereon.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal
Accountant (i) continue sending regular written notices to all AOs concerned at
least quarterly to demand settlement of their outstanding cash advances; (ii)
suspend/withhold the salaries of those AOs with unliquidated cash advances that
are still connected with the Municipality, taking into consideration the minimum
take home pay as provided in the General Appropriations Act; (iii) initiate filing
of appropriate action against AOs, who deliberately failed to liquidate their cash
advances after proper demand, if warranted; (iv) initiate the preparation and
consolidation of necessary documentation in support of the request for write-off
while waiting for the issuance of a new Circular for the extension of the filing
period; (v) investigate the reasons for negative balances and make appropriate
adjustments in the books to rectify discrepancies; and (vi) exert efforts in
retrieving the source documents of the unidentified Due from Officers and
Employees balance of ₱42,726.91 for proper disposition.

During the exit conference, the Municipal Accountant stated that demand letters
had been issued to the concerned AOs, and monthly salary deductions are being
implemented as refunds, particularly in the case of the disbursing officer.

With regard to the filing of write-off requests, the Management has noted several
challenges in securing death certificates from the Philippine Statistics Authority (PSA)
due to the Data Privacy Act, however, formal requests have already been sent to the
PSA. As to the unidentified balance of ₱42,726.91 under Due from Officers and
Employees account and the negative balances of some SLs, the Municipal Accountant
explained that identifying the AOs remains a challenge as some records were dated
back to year 2010 and were manually recorded.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) review and investigate
the accounts with abnormal/negative balances and draw necessary adjustments to
correct the respective account balances; (b) immediately reconcile the
unidentified accounts depending on the availability of reliable records; (c)
gather and prepare all relevant documents necessary for the filing of request for
write-off of dormant receivables amounting to ₱2,805,351.21 and observe the
guidelines and procedures set forth by COA Circular No. 2023-005; and (d)
immediately review the utilization reports submitted by the PGP and
accordingly record the liquidations in the books of accounts.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned
officials of Barangay Pasadeña to immediately liquidate and return the unused
balances of CY 2013 BDAF, if any.

The Municipal Accountant explained during the exit conference that the
abnormal or negative balances were caused by posting errors and that the necessary
adjustments had already been prepared. The JEV for the recording of the liquidation
report from the PGP will likewise be submitted to the Audit Team.

As for the CY 2013 BDAF, the Municipal Accountant confirmed that demand
letters had been issued to the concerned Barangay Pasadeña officials. The Management
also plans to request the Municipal Engineer to verify and inspect the implementation
90
and completion of the related project. If the project is found to be existing and complete,
refund of the unused balance will no longer be necessary.

We recommended that the: a. Municipal Accountant (i) accordingly record the reconciling items that need adjustment in the books; (ii) submit the DVs and the related supporting documents of the unrecorded check disbursements reported in the BRS; and (iii) formulate a clear cut-off policies on month-end closing with checklist of reports needed, e.g., cancelled and unreleased checks, from the Municipal Treasurer before finalizing the monthly BRS; b. Municipal Treasurer (i) provide the Audit Team with a proof that the DVs, ObRs and supporting documents of the unrecorded disbursements were already submitted to the Accounting Office, otherwise exhaust all means to locate and secure the missing transaction documents and immediately submit to the Municipal Accountant to facilitate recording thereof in the books of accounts; and (ii) ensure the reconciliation of the cashbooks with the subsidiary accounting records every end of the month or as the need arises in accordance with the provisions of Section 63(F) of the LTOM; and c. Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant reconcile their records as at December 31, 2024, take-up the necessary adjusting entries in their respective books and submit to the Audit Team a reconciliation statement thereon.
observations.managementAction: The reconciliation statements had already been submitted to the Municipal Accountant for his review. The identified reconciling items were already recorded in the cashbooks of the Municipal Treasurer, however, the corresponding entries had not yet been recorded by the Municipal Accountant in the books of accounts. The Municipal Accountant commented that the reconciliation report included reconciling items that were already adjusted in the books, thus he is asking for the updated reconciliation report from the Municipal Treasurer.
observations.reason: Although some reconciliations and adjustments were made (and discrepancies decreased compared to prior year), unreconciled balances and unrecorded reconciling items still remain as at year-end. The Municipal Accountant and Treasurer had not yet fully aligned their records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the Municipal Accountant to a. suspend or withhold the salaries of those AOs with unliquidated cash advances that are still connected with the Municipality, taking into consideration the minimum take home pay as provided in the General Appropriations Act; b. initiate filing of appropriate action against erring Accountable Officers; c. henceforth submit a monthly status of these cash advances to the Audit Team for monitoring of the Management’s action thereon until the cash advances have all been liquidated; d. immediately submit to the Audit Team the requested documents in connection with the unliquidated cash advances of the concerned AO for filing of appropriate case against them; e. exert efforts in retrieving the source documents of the unidentified Due from Officer and Employees balance of ₱42,726.91 to provide the Management with the necessary details and documents to undertake appropriate actions; and f. direct all AOs with unliquidated cash advances to submit a written justification for failure to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction: Demand letters had been issued to the concerned AOs, and monthly salary deductions are being implemented as refunds, particularly in the case of the disbursing officer. For the filing of write-off requests, Management has noted challenges in securing death certificates from the PSA due to privacy laws; formal requests have been sent to the PSA. For the unidentified balance of ₱42,726.91 under Due from Officers and Employees and negative balances, the Municipal Accountant explained identifying the AOs remains a challenge as some records dated back to 2010 and were manually recorded.
observations.reason: Some AOs have settled or are being recovered via salary deductions; however, certain accountabilities remain unliquidated, some are dormant over ten years, and documents for write-off and identification are incomplete or pending (e.g., death certificates, missing source documents).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) review and investigate the accounts with abnormal/negative balances and draw necessary adjustments to correct the respective account balances; (b) immediately reconcile the unidentified accounts depending on the availability of reliable records; (c) gather and prepare all relevant documents necessary for the filing of request for write-off of dormant receivables amounting to ₱2,805,351.21 and observe the guidelines and procedures set forth by COA Circular No. 2023-005; and (d) immediately review the utilization reports submitted by the PGP and accordingly record the liquidations in the books of accounts. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned officials of Barangay Pasadeña to immediately liquidate and return the unused balances of CY 2013 BDAF, if any.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant explained that the abnormal or negative balances were caused by posting errors and that the necessary adjustments had already been prepared. The JEV for the recording of the liquidation report from the PGP will likewise be submitted to the Audit Team. For the CY 2013 BDAF, demand letters had been issued to concerned Barangay Pasadeña. Management plans to request the Municipal Engineer to verify the project implementation; if project is existing and complete, refund may no longer be necessary.
observations.reason: Some adjustments prepared but not yet fully recorded; liquidation reports from PGP to be recorded; unresolved unidentified/dormant receivables require write-off procedures and further documentation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, thru the Municipal HRMO, in coordination with all the department heads, fast track the enhancement and revisions of the proposed OSSP of the Municipality taking into consideration its service requirements and financial capability, subject to the minimum standards and guidelines prescribed by the Civil Service Commission, to determine the appropriate human resource complement for their programs, projects and activities (PPAs) so that a reasonable number of Plantilla personnel may be established, while the need to hire COS personnel may be minimized.
observations.managementAction: Discussions had already been held with the Municipal Mayor on the revisions to the proposed OSSP based on the assessment in CY 2024. The updated proposal was already submitted to the Sangguniang Bayan for their review and approval.
observations.reason: The updated OSSP proposal was submitted to the SB but not yet approved as of reporting; COS hiring remains high pending finalization and approval of OSSP.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MSB (i) ensure strict and diligent compliance with the timely submission of SEF utilization reports to the concerned offices and oversight agencies for transparency and accountability pursuant to Item 6.1 of DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, series of 2017; and (ii) provide justification or documentation to support the signatory inconsistencies of the subject MSB resolutions.
observations.managementAction: The MSB convened a meeting to discuss this matter. Steps are being undertaken to address issues on the approving authority and to ensure compliance with the timely submission of SEF budget and utilization reports to the concerned government agencies.
observations.reason: Although local dissemination and posting occurred and the Municipal Accountant prepared utilization reports, the MSB did not submit quarterly reports to DepEd Central Office nor provided documentation to explain inconsistencies in MSB signatories; corrective steps being undertaken.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: a. LDRRM Council (i) include in the LDRRMFIP the list of PPAs to be charged against the prior years' unexpended LDRRMF; (ii) ensure that the LDRRMFIP is prepared in accordance with the format prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-002 and that PPAs integrated therein are included in the LDRRM Plan; (iii) review and evaluate whether the mentioned unimplemented programs and projects are still relevant, otherwise, consider reprogramming the unexpended funds to other DRRM priority projects; and (iv) ensure the timely implementation of all the planned priority projects during the budget year to strengthen the disaster preparedness capabilities of the Municipality; b. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer immediately reconcile their records on DRRMF in coordination with the MDRRM Officer to present the correct amounts in the NTFS, SCBAA, SAAOB and in the Monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of the DRRM Fund; c. Municipal Budget Officer, in coordination with the LDRRM Council, (i) provide the detailed breakdown of the budget and obligations per PPA and per thematic area duly reconciled with the LDRRM Plan; and (ii) ensure the alignment of the PPAs presented in the LDRRM Plan and integrated in the LDRRMFIP with the PPAs incorporated in the Annual Budget; and d. Municipal Accountant, record the necessary adjustments in the books to reflect the correct balance of the Trust Liabilities–DRRMF upon transfer of the savings from completed capital outlay projects.
observations.managementAction: Adjustments to the LDRRM Plan will be undertaken in the ensuing year to align with the updated LDRRMFIP while the reprogramming of unutilized prior years’ LDRRMF will also be considered. The Municipal Accountant committed to coordinate with the Municipal Budget Officer in reconciling their respective records. The SAAOB will be reconstructed to accurately reflect the continuing capital outlay projects.
observations.reason: Some adjustments and commitments to reconcile records and update plans were made, but significant inconsistencies remain across reports; certain PPAs remain unimplemented and STF balances unprogrammed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: a. Municipal Mayor, thru the Municipal Engineer, the MPDC, and the Municipal Accountant, (i) issue termination of contract to erring contractor who has negative slippage of ten percent (10%) or more in the completion of the work and cause the forfeiture of their performance security; (ii) require the imposition of liquidated damages from concerned contractors who incurred delays in the implementation of the projects; (iii) submit a written explanation for the deficiencies noted in audit and as to why the projects were not implemented despite the lapse of considerable time; (iv) henceforth take appropriate action from concerned contractors who incurred delays in the implementation of the project and ensure that the contract delivery time is strictly complied with; and (v) ensure that the infrastructure projects are well-planned and supported with complete detailed engineering before implementation; and b. Municipal Accountant and the BAC Secretariat immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking documents and henceforth strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001, RA No. 9184 and its updated 2016 R-IRR on the procurement of infrastructure projects.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer acknowledged the delays and reported corrective actions are being undertaken. Notices of Warning and Demand Letters were issued to concerned contractors, and the imposition of liquidated damages is being pursued. Management reported that terminating the contracts at this stage was deemed impractical; legal action and possible disqualification are being considered. Monthly meetings and capacity-building activities are being conducted to improve project implementation monitoring.
observations.reason: Corrective actions and warnings were issued but contracts were not terminated; some projects continued with remedial measures. Lacking technical documents were not all submitted; imposition of liquidated damages and termination actions remain in progress.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MDC (i) discontinue the practice of approving project proposals bearing generic terms; (ii) identify priority development projects for the unprogrammed funds; and (iii) submit justification for the funding of barangay roads under the 20% DF of the Municipality. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Direct the MPDC to (i) ensure that identified priority development projects are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready; (ii) ensure that the total project cost was already determine before proposed project approval; (iii) discontinue using multi-purpose hall, multi-purpose building and multi-purpose pavement in the project names as these are considered projects funded in lump sum; and (iv) standardize project names by ensuring complete details of the projects titles bearing complete technical specifications essential for its identification; b. Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to (i) assign project number to each PPA to easily identify the projects; (ii) discontinue the practice of splitting of budgets or funding the projects from multiple funds in multiple years; (iii) identify previously funded projects in lump-sum appropriations and rectify the identified projects through the SB’s approval; and (iv) present the individual PPAs per fund year in the SAAOB. c. Instruct the Municipal Engineer to ensure prompt implementation of the projects within the same budgetary year; and d. Direct the Municipal Accountant to (i) identify previously implemented projects sourced from multiple funds and implemented on a staggered basis and consolidate the same in one fund; (ii) process the cash transfer from 20% DF to GF-Proper of ₱9,519,464.80.
observations.managementAction: Management acknowledged that some BDAF projects, particularly road projects, were not fully implemented due to limited funding. Management committed to discontinue generic project titles, ensure future proposals are well-planned and cost-assessed, assign project numbers, avoid splitting budgets, and rectify previously lump-sum funded projects through SB approval. Municipal Engineer to review PPA reports to support timely implementation; Municipal Accountant to validate and consolidate staggered projects and process cash transfer from 20% DF to GF upon completion of validations.
observations.reason: Commitments made and some actions planned, but many projects remain unimplemented or unprogrammed; cash transfers and consolidation pending validation and reconciliation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC Chairperson, through the BAC Secretariat (a) immediately submit the lacking POs and supporting documents to the Audit Team to facilitate the necessary auditorial, legal, and technical review; and (b) henceforth, strictly adhere with COA Circular No. 2009-001 on the timely and complete submission of approved contracts together with their relevant supporting documents to the Audit Team to allow a timely and comprehensive audit thereof.
observations.managementAction: After the exit conference, the BAC Secretariat submitted the procurement documents to the Audit Team. The BAC Secretariat will strictly adhere to COA Circular No. 2009-001 and has reinforced internal controls through a document checklist, tracking system, and regular monitoring. Management provided a refresher orientation on procurement documentation and COA guidelines for the BAC Secretariat.
observations.reason: Initial delays in submission were remedied by subsequent submission of documents and instituted internal control measures; outstanding concerns addressed via submission.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: a. Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to implement the COEs by withholding payments due to the persons liable to immediately settle the audit disallowances otherwise payments thereto forth will be disallowed in audit; b. Municipal Treasurer submit the proof of his compliance with the enforcement of COEs and a report detailing the total amount already withheld from the persons liable; and c. Municipal Accountant (i) recognize in the books the audit disallowance amounting to ₱140,884.78 which has become final and executory and henceforth, ensure that all NDs covered with NFD are promptly recorded in the books of accounts; (ii) exhaust all means to locate and retrieve the proof of settlements of NDs and immediately submit these to the Audit Team for issuance of Notices of Settlement of Suspension/Disallowance/Charge; (iii) immediately complete the reconstruction of the SLs of the audit disallowances; and (iv) recognized settlement of accounts in the books upon receipt of NSSDC.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor will issue a memorandum directing the Municipal Treasurer to require the persons liable to settle the audit disallowances within a specified period; implementation includes withholding of salaries with due consideration to minimum take home pay. The Municipal Accountant prepared JEV for the disallowance of ₱140,884.78 and is reconstructing SLs via available ORs under the Municipal Treasurer; where documents cannot be recovered, management plans to direct settlements from the persons liable.
observations.reason: Some actions taken (demand letters, JEV prepared) and partial settlements recorded, but proof of settlements (ORs/JEVs) not all recovered/submitted; some disallowances remain unsettled and COEs enforcement documentation pending.

observations.managementAction: The Municipality budgeted and implemented various GAD PPAs during the year.
No ODA received for CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, with inputs from the Municipal Engineer, introduce improvements to the current final disposal facility to conform with minimum considerations for operating a sanitary landfill; implement segregation of solid wastes at source; revisit and revise the SWMP to conform with proposed improvements of the current dumpsite; and immediately implement the projects in the revised SWMP especially on the establishment of the new dumpsite.
observations.managementAction: Management revised the SWMP which has been approved by the SWM Board and is awaiting approval from the PCSDS. The ME and LGU are implementing segregation programs and improvements including construction of a perimeter fence and washing area for garbage collectors; partnership with BLGU in implementing a 'no segregation, no collection' policy and conducting inspections.
observations.reason: Some physical improvements and policy enforcement initiated; revised SWMP prepared but awaiting external approval; full implementation of new dumpsite and all SWMP projects pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Petty Cash Custodians to present the liquidation documents for proper recording in the books, otherwise require immediate settlement of their accountabilities by returning the balance of cash on hand to the Municipal Treasurer.
observations.managementAction: The Petty Cash Custodian has liquidated PCF inactive balances and returned funds; reconciliation actions were undertaken.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, via a memorandum, encourage the delinquent municipal officials and employees and the elected barangay officials with RPT delinquencies to immediately settle their obligations, promoting efficiency and effectiveness in generating revenues. The memorandum should be supported by a schedule of updated amounts as determined by the Municipal Treasurer.
observations.managementAction: A memorandum was sent to encourage settlement of RPT delinquencies; updated schedules prepared by the Municipal Treasurer and notices issued to concerned officials.
observations.reason: Some delinquencies settled after reminders; others remain unpaid and further follow-up required.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. require the Municipal Accountant to (i) verify the validity of payables aged two years and more and check existence of actual claimants by sending confirmation letters; (ii) process payments of valid claims or initiate reversion of unconfirmed payables to the unappropriated surplus; (iii) trace and retrieve supporting documents for SLs with insufficient details; and (iv) analyze Accounts Payable and effect correcting entries to reclassify SLs to appropriate liability accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant started adjustments and reconciliation of accounts payable and initiated verification efforts for older balances. Some adjustments recorded; further reconciliation ongoing.
observations.reason: Reconciliation ongoing; some dormant payables require confirmation or reversal; documentation retrieval incomplete and adjustments pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor in coordination with the Municipal Accountant and GSO: c. transfer functions of fuel requisitions, monitoring and reporting from each department to the GSO; d. formulate a sound internal control on fuel procurement, requisition and reporting including establishment of standard fuel usage per vehicle, conduct orientation to stakeholders on policies and sanctions, and finalize policies, forms and frequency of reporting.
observations.managementAction: A fuel depot was established in CY2024; GASOLINE control centralized in the GSO. Draft policies and procedures were prepared; GSO drafted policies and guidelines and submitted to the Municipal Mayor. Fuel consumption monitoring centralized and inspections conducted.
observations.reason: Centralization of fuel requisition and some policy drafting completed; final policies and full implementation (including verified fuel consumption reports and reconciled supplier records) still pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer (a) submit certified copies of registries for Continuing Appropriations as of Dec 31, 2021; (b) strictly comply with MNGAS provisions and ensure preparation of registries with project details and amounts in accordance with authorized appropriation ordinances; and that Municipal Accountant and MBO review variances and make necessary adjustments and comply with monthly reconciliation of allotments.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant adjusted the CY 2022 SCBAA. Municipal Budget Officer will review and reconcile the report with records; efforts to comply with registries and reconciliation ongoing.
observations.reason: Some adjustments made but variances persist; full reconciliation and submission of registries pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the OIC-MPDC in coordination with the Municipal Mayor ensure preparation of 5-year Business Plan for each LEE/PU; set reasonable revenue targets and implement corrective measures where revenues deviate; consider options if LEEs cannot fully fund operating expenses.
observations.managementAction: The MEEDO, with assistance from USAID and Safe Water project, started preparing business plans for LEEs; however assistance was stopped and finalization deferred.
observations.reason: Preparation of business plans initiated but not completed due to stoppage of external assistance; revenue-targeting and other reforms pending.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the:
Inventory Committee (i) immediately complete the Report on the Physical Count of PPE and Report on the Physical Count of the Local Road Network System and reconcile these reports with the Property Cards, PPE Ledger Cards, Road Network Ledger Cards and other accounting records; and (ii) submit a copy thereof to the Audit Team; and

Municipal Engineer (i) submit a complete report on the costs, description and specifications and cost segregation of the road components to the Municipal Accountant as bases for disclosures in the NTFS; and (ii) regularly assess each road component for impairment.
observations.managementAction:
Inventory process is still on-going. 





The Municipal Engineer have been directed to complete and submit the required report immediately.
observations.reason:
No Inventory Report as of yearend was submitted.

Reiterated in Finding No. 7 of this AAR





No submitted report. The Municipal Engineer did not finish the report on time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer immediately submit to the Audit Team the details of reconciling items totaling ₱433,946,734.53, review the reconciling items totaling ₱32,779,330.03 and investigate the variances of ₱17,246,091.50 noted in the Reconciliation Statement of Actual Amounts.
observations.reason: No submission of reconciliation report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant immediately complete the
reconciliation of their records as of December 31, 2021 and submit the
reconciliation statement thereon and take-up the necessary adjusting entries in
their respective records;
b. Municipal Accountant (i) record the reconciling items that need adjustment in
the books including the adjustment of CIB and payables balances due to stale
checks; and (ii) immediately submit the original bank statements and paid
checks pertaining to the unrecorded disbursements reported in the BRS; and
c. Municipal Treasurer (i) provide the Audit Team with proof that the DVs,
ObRs and supporting documents of the unrecorded disbursements are already
submitted to the Accounting Office, otherwise immediately submit to the
Municipal Accountant the original source documents thereof, after which, the
same shall be submitted to the Audit Team for audit purposes; (ii) account all
unclaimed checks which had become stale for immediate cancellation; (iii) and
strictly observe the provisions of Section 63 of the LTOM on the maintenance
of complete and updated cashbooks for all cash accounts of all funds and
reconciliation of cashbook balances with subsidiary accounting records.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (a) deposit all collections
promptly and intact with the AGDB in compliance with Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445,
Section 2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 32 of the MNGAS for LGUs,
Volume I; and (b) submit to the Audit Team a satisfactory written explanation on the
noted lapses in the cash management of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal review the related reports to determine
the cause/s of the variances between the RPT and SET collections as well as of their
year-end receivable balances and adjust the affected accounts, as appropriate.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Accountant (a) serve written notices
to all concerned accountable officers requiring immediate settlement of their
outstanding cash advances and institute legal action against them, if
warranted;
b. Municipal Mayor, HRM Officer and the Municipal Accountant submit
immediately to the Audit Team the requested documents in connection with
the unliquidated CAs of five AOs mentioned in Table 4.5 for the filing of
appropriate case against them;
c. Municipal Accountant:
 prepare the schedule of all receivables, unliquidated CAs, and fund
transfers pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19,
2016 and COA Memorandum No. 2017-010 dated May 15, 2017;
 enforce strict monitoring of grant and liquidation of CAs to ensure that no
additional CA is granted unless the previous one has been duly liquidated
and accounted for; and
 immediately submit to the Audit Team the SLs for the cash advances of
officers and employees totaling ₱1,267,673.41 as of December 31, 2021; and
d. Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Accountant ensure that each provisions
of COA Circular No. 97-002 are strictly observed and that all CAs are fully
liquidated at the end of the year. Consider adopting the “no liquidation, no
cash advance” policy to forestall accumulation of unliquidated balances.

If the probability of liquidation is remote, such as death of the accountable
officer, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor file a request for authority to
write-off dormant receivable accounts in accordance with the guidelines provided in
COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert more efforts to
immediately reconcile the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts
in all funds, determine the cause/s of disparity in the balances and record the
necessary adjustments in the books to present their correct balances in the financial
statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of all supplies and
materials and prepare the report thereon, reconcile the results of the count
with available records in the Accounting and General Services Offices (GSO)
and submit the inventory report to the Audit Team for verification;
b. MDRRMO, MSWDO, GSO, MEEDO, MEO and other concerned offices to (i)
immediately submit the required distribution/consumption/utilization reports
of inventories and proof of receipts for the relief goods given to various
beneficiaries as well as the inventory of undistributed/unissued goods/items;
and (ii) render satisfactory explanation on the delayed reporting of the result
of distribution in connection with the COVID-19 operations of the LGU and of
the utilization of other supplies and materials under various offices;
c. Municipal GSO (i) ensure that issuances of supplies and materials are
supported with duly signed RIS to establish accountability on the part of the
end-users, particularly those departments assigned to the distribution of relief
goods; and (ii) submit to the Municipal Accountant the monthly summary of
supplies and materials issued duly supported by the RIS as basis for recording
expenses; and
d. Municipal Accountant, upon submission of the required summary report and
distribution list and the inventory report by the concerned offices, make the
necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balances of the Inventory
accounts.

We recommended that the:
a. Inventory Committee (i) immediately complete the Report on the Physical
Count of PPE and the Report on the Physical Count of the Local Road
Network System and reconcile these reports with the Property Cards, PPE
Ledger Cards, Road Network Ledger Cards and other accounting records;
and (ii) submit a copy thereof to the Audit Team for verification;
b. Municipal Accountant (i) reconcile the year-end balances of the PPE and the
differences of total acquisition costs of PPE account balances reported in the
financial statements and in the PPE schedules; and (ii) recompute and provide
for the correct accumulated depreciation of the aforementioned PPE; and
c. Municipal Engineer (i) submit a complete report on the costs, description and
specifications and cost segregation of the road components to the Municipal
Accountant as bases for disclosures in the NTFS; and (ii) regularly assess each
road component for impairment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (i) require the Inventory
Committee to complete the one-time cleansing of the PPE accounts of the LGU; and
(ii) submit the documents in relation to the implementation of the one-time cleansing
as requeseted by the Audit Team under its letter dated February 7, 2022.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. thru the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development
Coordinator (MPDC), (i) ensure that future projects funded by the NGAs
are implemented within the agreed project schedule and strictly comply with
the prescribed procedures and guidelines related to implementation of the
projects; and (ii) apprise the Audit Team of the action taken by the
Management on the unfinished contract by the defaulting contractor;
b. secure copies of MOA entered into and signed in behalf of MGEN and all its
annexes and supporting documents including confirmation letters from
source agencies indicating grant and purpose of the funds transfer and
furnish the department heads or officials in-charge in implementing the
projects and the Municipal Accountant copies of such documents prior to
implementation to ensure that the trust funds are utilized efficiently in
accordance with the trust agreements and implementing guidelines; and
c. thru the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer, collect/recover
from the contractor of the Local Access Road the amount of ₱98,376.66
representing the portion of the advance payment not yet recouped from the
progress billings.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant carefully review the
total utilization of the trust receipts to avoid excessive refund to the source agency
and/or national treasury.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other concerned personnel
render justification on the noted observations on the appointment of the Municipal
Government Assistant Department Head I under the Office of the Municipal
Administrator.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Budget Officer (i) submit certified copies of registries for
Continuing Appropriations as of December 31, 2021; (ii) strictly comply with
the requirements of Section 18 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume 2, and ensure
that the preparation of registries, particularly the names of projects or object
of expenditures and amount of appropriations, are in accordance with the
authorized appropriation ordinances and augmentations; and
b. Municipal Accountant and the MBO (i) review the noted variances and make
necessary adjustments to correct the total final budgets and actual amounts of
the CY 2021 SCBAA; and (ii) henceforth, strictly compy with the provisions of
Section 10, Chapter 1 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, on the monthly
reconciliation of their records on allotment available for appropriation.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Mayor, Sangguniang Bayan members, MDRRMO and the local
development council, ETDF Management Board and the MBO: i) ensure
timely submission and approval of the proposed investment plans and budgets
in accordance with the provisions of R.A. No. 7160 and the guidelines of the
Budget Operations Manual, 2016 Edition, on budget process as well as the
forms prescribed by the said Manual;
b. MBO and the Sanggunian provide explanation on the noted deficiency on
funding source of ₱31,693,640.59 for Supplemental Budget No. 1, s-2021;
c. Municipal Mayor, MBO and MPDC indicate the object of expenditures or
present the PPAs in detail per fund in the Annual Budget for proper
utilization and monitoring of the funds; and
d. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer ensure that the
certification of savings or fund sources of realignment are provided with
detailed breakdown of savings and items of appropriations affected by the
changes in the annual budget.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Accountant and the MBO immediately reconcile their records on
DRRMF in coordination with the MDRRM Officer;
b. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer cause the immediate
transfer of the remaining unexpended 2021 DRRMF to the LDRRMF STF
bank account;
c. MDRRM Officer immediately submit to the Municipal Accountant the proof
of distribution and/or consumption report of the welfare goods for the
adjustment of the Inventory and Trust Liability-DRRMF accounts; and
d. Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjustments in the TF books for
the completed infrastructure projects and for the consumed/distributed
welfare goods once the report is submitted by the MDRRM Officer.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Mayor, thru the MDRRMO and the MHO, (i) submit written
justification on why the LGU resorted to contracting with private hotels/inn
instead of using government facilities; and (ii) exercise prudence in the
utilization of funds for COVID19 response operation and consider utilizing the
municipal evacuation centers and other government-owned buildings as
quarantine and isolation facility for COVID-19 patients to avoid incurrence of
unnecessary rental fees;
b. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) (i) render justification, duly supported
with documentation, on the noted deficiencies and inconsistencies in the
procurement of rental or room accommodations and COVID-19 testing kits;
(ii) ensure that the bid offer or price quotation of the supplier and the
Purchase Order contain detailed specification of items being procured; (iii)
observe due diligence in the determination of price estimates of goods and
services prior to commencing procurement; and (iv) exert all efforts to secure
the most advantageous price for the LGU;
c. MBO (i) submit written explanation on the transactions paid even without
valid appropriations; and (ii) in all instances, verify the validity and
availability of appropriations before affixing his signature in the Obligation
Request;
d. Municipal Accountant (i) thoroughly check the completeness and propriety of
the supporting documents of the disbursements as well as the liquidations for
cash advances granted; and (ii) immediately record in the books and submit
the disbursement voucher and the supporting documents of the isolation
facility rental paid amounting to ₱399,400.00; and
e. Municipal Mayor, thru the MBO and the Local Development Council, strictly
comply with the guidelines laid down by the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1
dated November 4, 2020 on the utilization of the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Mayor, thru the Municipal Engineer, stop the practice of allowing
pakyaw labor for hauling of aggregates needed in the project implementation;
b. Municipal Engineer (i) estimate a reasonable price for aggregates based on the
prevailing market price; (ii) prepare the POW with complete costing such that
the quantity and cost of aggregates and fuel requirement are included in the
approved budget; (iii) immediately submit the fuel consumption and
equipment utilization reports and other lacking technical documents; (iv)
exclude the construction tools and equipment in the project cost estimate in
the POW and instead, request for separate purchase of tools and equipment
needed in the project implementation, procurement of which should be based
on the current available tools of the MEO;
c. Municipal Accountant (i) ensure that payments for the procurement of fuel,
oil and lubricants are supported with proof of delivery; and (ii) immediately
reclassify the completed projects to the appropriate PPE accounts;
d. Municipal Accountant and the MBO reconcile their records to determine the
correct total obligations and actual cost incurred for each project; and
e. MBO explain the unreported obligation of ₱999,343.80 in the CY 2020
SCBAA for the Construction of Fish Port, Bgy. Corong-corong (funded by CY
2017 Continuing Appropriations) and submit the authenticated copy of its
registry.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Engineer (ME), (i) issue termination
of contract to erring contractors with negative slippage of ten percent (10%)
or more in the completion of work pursuant to Section III-A(2) of the
Guidelines on Termination of Contracts; (ii) cause the forfeiture of their
performance security pursuant to Section 8.5, Annex E of the Revised IRR of
R.A. No. 9184; (iii) thru the BAC, consider issuing a Blacklisting Order in
accordance with the Uniform Guidelines For Blacklisting of Manufacturers,
Suppliers, Distributors, Contractors and Consultants provided in Appendix 17
of the said I.R.R; and (iv) provide the Audit Team a copy of the notice of
termination, forfeiture of the performance security and blacklisting order duly
received by the defaulting contractor;
b. Municipal Accountant and the ME submit the (i) status report of all
infrastructure projects of the municipality supported with SWA to facilitate
verification of the reported project accomplishments; and (ii) recompute the
correct liquidated damages and recover the uncollected LDs from concerned
contractors;
c. ME and the MPDC (i) fast track the implementation of infrastructure projects
and ensure that all projects are strictly monitored and implemented in
accordance with plans and schedules; and (ii) for projects not implemented
within the schedule, validate the project status, and prepare inspection report
and SWA as of the target date of completion or contract expiry date; and
d. Municipal Mayor, ME and MPDC strictly comply with the guidelines of COA
Circular No. 2013-004 regarding information and publicity of PPAs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, thru the BAC, (i) render
justification on the noted deficiencies; (ii) strictly comply with the requirements of
R.A. No. 9184 and other relevant laws, rules, and regulations on procurement; and
(iii) for all procurements, ensure that the LGU negotiate only with legally, technically,
and financially capable suppliers and exert all efforts to secure the most
advantageous price for the LGU.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Mayor, thru the MEEDO, cause the preparation of the 5-year
Business Plan for each LEE/PU in accordance with the Manual on the
Operations of LEEs; (ii) thru the Local Finance Committee and MEEDO,
project reasonable target revenue in each LEE and implement corrective
measures in case actual revenues have negative deviations from the
target/budget; and (iii) when the LEEs/PUs income cannot fully fund their
operating expenses, consider the options provided in Item 3.4 of the DBM
LBC No. 100 dated October 1, 2012;
b. MBO and the Municipal Treasurer (i) ensure that obligations are backed up
by realized revenues and that cash is actually available for payment of
expenditures;
c. Municipal Accountant make necessary adjustments in the GF proper and
LEE books to reflect the correct financial position of each LEE/PUs; and
d. Municipal Treasurer ensure that only properly bonded regular/permanent
employees are designated as collecting officers to safeguard the municipal
government’s funds.

We recommended that the a) Municipal Accountant immediately complete the
reconciliation of their records on Due to BIR, GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth to
determine the cause/s of the negative/unremitted balances and provide subsidiary
schedules for balances totaling ₱136,740.09 to facilitate remittance thereof to the
concerned agencies and to determine and record the necessary adjustments in the
books; and b) Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer ensure that funds
collected by the municipal government in behalf of other government agencies are
remitted to them fully within the prescribed due dates to avoid incurrence of interests
and penalties.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Mayor (i) require the immediate
settlement of audit disallowances; and (ii) enforce the COEs by requiring the
Municipal Treasurer to withhold payment of salaries and any amount due to the
persons liable; (b) Municipal Treasurer show proof of his compliance to the
enforcement of COEs and provide details of the total amount already withheld from
the persons liable; and (c) Municipal Accountant (i) reconstruct and immediately
submit to the Audit Team the SLs for audit disallowances totaling ₱759,824.50; and
(ii) transmit the proof of settlements of NDs to the Audit Team for issuance of Notices
of Settlement of Suspension/Disallowance/Charge.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as head of the GAD Focal Point
System (GFPS), require the:
a. GFPS to (i) strictly observe the prescribed timelines for GAD Planning and
Budgeting, as provided in PCW-DBM-DILG-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as
amended, to facilitate timely preparation and submission of the GPB to the
PPDO and the subsequent review and endorsement by the DILG Provincial
Office to ensure gender responsiveness of the GAD programs, projects and
activities; (ii) submit to the Audit Team the supporting documents to the GAD
AR and henceforth ensure that the same is prepared and submitted within the
prescribed period; and (iii) provide explanation for the differences noted
between the amount of GAD budget and GAD PPAs presented in the GPB,
GAD AR and annual budget; and
b. MPDC, with the assistance of the GFPS, establish a complete and updated
GAD database, and conduct gender analysis to serve as a basis for addressing
gender issues and concerns in GAD planning and budgeting for the ensuing
year.

We recommended that the:
Local Chief Executive (LCE) 
require the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to immediately reconcile their records as of December 31, 2020, submit a reconciliation statement thereon and take-up the necessary adjusting entries in their respective records; and 

demand from/require the Municipal Treasurer to immediately submit to the Municipal Accountant the disbursement vouchers, obligation requests and supporting documents of the unrecorded disbursements, after which, the same shall be submitted to the Auditor for audit purposes;

Municipal Treasurer 
strictly observe the provisions of Section 63 of the LTOM on the required maintenance of complete and updated cashbooks for all cash accounts of all funds and reconciliation of cashbook balances with subsidiary accounting records as this is an important control tool for cash; and 
account all unclaimed checks which had been outstanding for over six months from date of issue wherein the original DVs and supporting documents are still in the custody of the treasury office and cancel these stale checks; and 

Municipal Accountant
prepare the BRS for the two savings accounts maintained by the LGU; 
immediately record the transactions upon submission of the lacking vouchers and supporting documents; and
ensure that all reconciling items are supported with documents to serve as basis in making the necessary adjustments in the books.
observations.managementAction:

There was partial submission of reconciliation reports by the Municipal Treasurer.

is taking much time since the discrepancies dated years back prior to 2010 when the LGU files were not yet saved in electronic copies and the personnel performing the reconciliation had to manually retrieve the files.

Stale checks in CY 2021 totaling ₱311,752.61 are still unadjusted.




Reconciling items in CY 2021 aggregating ₱958,228.61 were not yet adjusted/ recorded.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:
Municipal Treasurer
prepare separate reports of collections for RPT and SET and automatically deposit the proceeds of the additional 1% RPT accruing to the SEF depository account, in compliance with Section 272 of R.A. 7160 and Section 64(A) of the LTOM; and 


transfer the SET collections from advance payments which were improperly deposited in the GF account to the SEF depository account;

Municipal Accountant:
review the related reports to determine the cause/s of the discrepancy between the RPT and SET collections amounting to ₱34,629,692.37 and adjust the affected accounts, as appropriate; and
reconcile the recorded RPT Receivable, SET Receivable, Deferred RPT Income and Deferred SET Income accounts with the Treasury report on the tax delinquencies for the current and prior years.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:

Municipal Budget Officer:
submit certified copies of registries for Continuing Appropriations and Special Education Fund as of December 31, 2020; 


exercise due care in preparing SAAOB and make sure that only those expenditures incurred are included in the total obligations for the budget year; 


strictly comply with the requirements of Section 18 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume 2 and ensure that the preparation of the registries, particularly the names of projects or object of expenditures and amount of appropriations, are in accordance with the authorized appropriation ordinances; and 

prepare the obligation request in accordance with the format and instructions provided by COA Circular No. 2006-02 and specify the particular function/program/project per authorized budget to which the obligation is being charged; and

Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer: 
reconcile their records on allotments and obligations monthly, verify resulting discrepancies and submit the required budget reports and reconciliation statement within the prescribed period; and 
discontinue the practice of allowing non-capital outlay appropriations to be continued in the succeeding year.

Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer ensure that the assistance to the Barangays are paid thru issuance of check under the name of the Barangay and duly supported with official receipts issued by the Barangay Treasurer; and
Municipal Mayor (i) allot portion of the budget for medical cash assistance to the Municipal Health Office for procurement of medicines and vaccines; (ii) strengthen free medical services like free consultation, medicines, dental, laboratory and vaccines by providing the Municipal Health Office with complete medical facilities and adequate supplies for better delivery of services to the general public; and (ii) prevent the incurrence of unnecessary expenditures as defined under Sections 4.1 and 6.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-003 by ensuring that all expenditures are incurred only for specific purposes that are responsive to the exigencies of the service and supportive of the implementation of the objectives and mission of the Municipality.
